articles.title: Debt Snowball vs. Avalanche: Which Payoff Method Actually Saves You More
articles.dek: Both methods put every spare dollar toward debt. The difference is which debt gets that dollar first — and it changes both how much interest you pay and how fast it feels like you're winning.

articles.title: Student Loan Repayment Changed July 2026: What RAP Means for Your Payment
articles.dek: SAVE, PAYE, and ICR are gone for new borrowers, replaced by two plans: RAP and the Tiered Standard Plan. Here's how each one calculates your payment, with a real worked example.

articles.title: The New 401(k) Roth Catch-Up Rule: What Changes for High Earners in 2026
articles.dek: If you're 50 or older and earned more than $150,000 last year, your 401(k) catch-up contributions just lost their upfront tax deduction. Here's exactly how the new rule works.

articles.title: Why Credit Card Rates Are Still Near 21% After Three Fed Rate Cuts
articles.dek: The Fed has cut its benchmark rate three times since 2025, but the average credit card still charges close to 21% — near an all-time high. Here's why, and what actually lowers your cost.

articles.title: Mortgage Rates Stuck Near 6.5% in 2026 — What the New Housing Law Does
articles.dek: Rates aren't expected to move much for the rest of the year. A sweeping new housing bill became law anyway, aimed at a different problem entirely: how many homes actually get built.

articles.title: India's New Income Tax Act 2025: Slabs, Rebate & What Changes
articles.dek: India's Income Tax Act 2025 takes effect April 1, 2026. Here's the new-regime slab structure, how the ₹12.75 lakh tax-free threshold actually works, and what it means for your filing.

articles.title: ECB Raises Rates for the First Time Since 2023: What It Means
articles.dek: The ECB hiked rates in June 2026, its first increase since 2023, with another decision due July 24. Here's why, what's next, and what it means for eurozone mortgages and savings.

articles.title: Africa's Interest Rates Are Moving in Opposite Directions in 2026
articles.dek: Nigeria is holding at 26.5%, Kenya is cutting toward 8.75%, and South Africa just hiked to 7%. Here's why Africa's biggest economies are diverging on rates, and what it means for borrowers and savers.
